title: Before They Became: A Tale of Banjhakri and Lamlamey
cover: /covers/Before They Became - A Tale of Banjhakri and Lamlamey.PNG

chapters.title: Chapter 1: Of Light and Love
chapters.content:
Come here. Come close. Let me tell you a story you think you've already heard  - the tale of Banjhakri and Lamlamey.

But stories are not always what they seem. Before they were names spoken in hushed voices, before they became something the forest kept; they were only a boy and a girl.
A story of friendship. Of love.
You may have heard it before, passed down in whispers from your grandparents, shaped and reshaped with every telling.  But this one, this one is my grandmother's. The one she grew up listening to, in the dense forest villages of Dingla.

So come here.
Come close.

In the late 1700s, by the Arun Valley, there was a settlement that clung to the curve of the river as if it had always belonged there. Houses of stone and mud rested along the hillside, their roofs darkened by rain and time. Thin trails wound between them; narrow paths shaped not by planning, but by footsteps repeated over generations.

Below, the Arun moved steadily, never in a hurry, its voice so constant that the villagers no longer heard it……until it was gone.

In the mornings, mist rose from the water and settled over the fields, softening the world before the sun burned it away. By afternoon, the hills stood clear and green, and on certain days, far beyond them, the white peaks of Mount Everest and Makalu appeared; distant, unmoving, like something that belonged to another world.

The forest began where the fields ended. Not suddenly, but enough that people knew where not to go. And when night fell, it fell completely. No lamps reached beyond the last house. No voices carried past the edge of the village.

Only the river remained and whatever listened from the dark.

Most of the people in the village were Kirati people, deeply rooted in their belief in the spirits of the forest. Others lived among them too, families who farmed the land and followed different ways of life. Among them were the Chhetris. They lived side by side, in a kind of quiet harmony but love did not cross those boundaries.

In that village was a boy born into a family of shamans, though his heart was drawn elsewhere. He was named Kiran, for he was born on the first day of spring, after a long and bitter winter. Kingdoms were shifting. A nation was slowly taking shape. And in the quiet of night, the boy would whisper to himself before sleep -  Kiran Bahadur Rai… a soldier. He had seen them; men crossing the valley with purpose, with steel, with something larger than the village in their eyes. He wanted that.

And yet, when spring returned and the hills turned green again, Kiran could always be found by the river, playing his murali; as if the forest listened, and the wind carried it further than he ever could.

A few houses away stood a different kind of household, one known for its sons. Warriors, the villagers said. Men who would one day give their lives for their kingdom. And yet, in that house, after five sons, a girl was born. Her mother, with her last breath, named her Maya. Maya Chhetri. Her brothers guarded her closely, never letting her wander near the edge of the forest. She did not play like the other children. She did not run as far.
And so, quietly, she began to dream - not of duty, but of freedom.
Maya, who was rarely allowed beyond the courtyard, learned to notice the smallest things; the way wildflowers grew between stones, how their colours changed with the light.

By spring and summer, her hands were never empty. She wove what she could find into crowns and small garlands. As if making something of the world she was not allowed to wander.

Girls were married young in those days. Sometimes as young as five. It was only a ritual but even rituals had weight. Maya was eight, and the threads around her wrist had been tied before she was old enough to ask why. She knew, in the quiet way children know things no one explains, that one day she would leave her home, her brothers, her hills.
And she would leave him too.

The boy by the river. The one who asked too many questions. Who made her crowns of wildflowers.
Who brought her chestnuts, berries, and wild pears. Who led her, sometimes, just close enough to the forest edge where the kaphal trees grew. Never further. When she grew quiet, he would play the murali to make her smile again.

And so they were, two children from different worlds.
One who dreamed of flying free, and one who dreamed of dying for something greater than himself.
